5.  {"id":"ft-xbnl0.5","title":"[EPIC] Release readiness, operator polish, and permanent regression guards","description":"# Goal
Turn the completed technical work into a ship-ready, operator-trustworthy project: final docs, guards, tuning guidance, end-to-end acceptance scenarios, and a closure bundle that future contributors can rely on.

This epic exists because a project can be technically improved and still not be finish-line ready if docs lie, CI does not defend the invariants, or no one has captured the evidence and workflows that define ready for real multi-agent use.","design":"# Design
1. Rewrite the narrative around the final native architecture and honest supported behavior.
2. Add permanent machine checks for the invariants this program establishes.
3. Expose live health and incident-diagnostics surfaces so operators can actually use the reliability work under pressure.
4. Polish first-run setup, environment validation, and recovery entrypoints so the finished system is operable from the first real interaction.
5. Validate the original mission workflows end to end, not just unit by unit.
6. Package the result into a final closure bundle with explicit remaining risks, if any.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Docs and operator guidance describe the final system rather than a migration-in-progress story.
2. CI, doctor, and audit surfaces fail fast when `tokio`, `smol`, or fake supported capabilities creep back in.
3. Operator acceptance scenarios covering the original mission are executed with artifacts.
4. A final release-candidate checklist and evidence bundle exist in bead-native form.
5. Operator-facing health and diagnostic workflows are first-class outputs of this epic, not incidental notes.
6. First-run setup and recovery entrypoints are polished enough to be part of the supported operator story rather than an undocumented initiation ritual.","notes":"This epic is what prevents the repo from sliding back into technically interesting but operationally ambiguous. It closes the loop between implementation, proof, and operator trust.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:52.840501Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-03T20:09:15.048719Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","docs","goal-line","operator","release-readiness"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:52.840501Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","title":"Rewrite README, AGENTS, and operator docs around the final native Asupersync architecture and honest support matrix","description":"# Goal
Make the repository narrative match the finished system.

This task exists because docs that still describe migration debt, placeholder capabilities, or outdated runtime assumptions will immediately undermine the credibility of the finish-line push.","design":"# Design
1. Rewrite the high-level architecture story around the final native Asupersync model.
2. Update AGENTS guidance and operator docs to reflect the real supported capability matrix, verification expectations, and live health or diagnostic surfaces added by this program.
3. Remove or rewrite stale references to transitional architecture that no longer exists.
4. Keep the docs specific enough that future agents can work from them without reopening old planning documents.","acceptance_criteria":"1. README, AGENTS, and core operator docs describe the final system rather than a migration in progress.
2. Runtime, support-matrix, and verification language is accurate and current.
3. Operators and future agents can understand the intended architecture and supported surfaces directly from the docs.
4. The updated docs are cross-linked to the finish-line evidence model where appropriate.
5. Documentation examples, support claims, and operator procedures are validated against the shipped commands, recorded verification artifacts, live diagnostic surfaces, and the first-run or recovery path so the docs cannot drift ahead of reality.","notes":"This bead is the documentation counterpart to the code cleanup. If the narrative stays stale, the project will feel unfinished even after the code improves.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:22:31.852785Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","updated_at":"2026-04-03T20:08:16.414627Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","docs","goal-line","operator"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:41:05.535955Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:23:42.715747Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.3.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:23:43.022385Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:23:43.471912Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:22:31.852785Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:41:09.776559Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T20:08:14.259339Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"ft-xbnl0.5.2","title":"Add permanent CI, doctor, and audit guards for zero tokio smol and zero fake supported capabilities","description":"# Goal
Make the finish-line invariants self-defending.

This task exists because the project needs durable protection against two classes of regression: runtime debt creeping back in and fake supported capabilities quietly reappearing.","design":"# Design
1. Add or extend CI checks, doctor surfaces, and audit scripts that defend the no-Tokio no-Smol property and the honest support-matrix property.
2. Keep the checks actionable so failures explain what violated the rule and where.
3. Wire the checks into the normal contributor path instead of leaving them as optional extras.
4. Reuse the evidence and inventories built earlier in the program where possible.
5. Validate not only code invariants but also the verification contract itself where practical, including required artifact manifests, log schema, and deterministic E2E entrypoints.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The key finish-line invariants are defended by permanent automated or semi-automated checks.
2. Contributors can run the relevant checks locally and in CI.
3. Violations are specific enough to fix quickly.
4. The release lane can trust these guards as part of ongoing maintenance.
5. Selected deterministic E2E scripts, artifact manifests, and structured-log expectations are automatically checked or linted so the testing and evidence discipline in this program does not silently decay.
6. This bead follows `ft-xbnl0.1.4`'s shared verification contract: comprehensive unit coverage for touched code paths, at least one deterministic integration or E2E script with detailed timestamped logging and artifact capture, and exact `rch exec -- ...` verification commands recorded in the completion evidence.","notes":"This bead is how the finish-line work stays finished after the first release candidate.","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:22:32.309029Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","updated_at":"2026-04-16T04:28:22.788352Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","ci","doctor","goal-line","quality-gate"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.2","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:41:05.793483Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.2","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:23:43.811867Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.2","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.3.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:23:44.145075Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.2","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:22:32.309029Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.5.3","title":"Define blessed tuning profiles and operator playbooks for 10, 50, and 200 plus pane fleets","description":"# Goal
Turn the performance and reliability findings into concrete operating guidance.

This task exists because a finished swarm terminal should not leave operators guessing which settings, limits, and practices are appropriate for small, medium, and very large fleets.","design":"# Design
1. Derive tuning profiles from the reliability and soak evidence instead of speculation.
2. Document the practical knobs, warning signs, diagnostic surfaces, and recommended operating patterns for 10, 50, and 200 plus pane deployments.
3. Keep the guidance honest about which workloads are supported and which are still aspirational.
4. Package the result so future operators can use it without reading the whole backlog.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The project has concrete tuning guidance for representative small, medium, and large fleet sizes.
2. The guidance is tied to measured evidence rather than vague rules of thumb.
3. Operators can use the playbooks and diagnostic surfaces to configure and monitor the system responsibly.
4. The playbooks are integrated with the final docs lane.
5. The playbooks cite exact verification scripts, thresholds, logged artifacts, and escalation paths instead of vague advice.","notes":"This bead turns performance work into something that operators can actually act on.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:22:32.737574Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","updated_at":"2026-04-03T17:44:50.789191Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["goal-line","operator","performance","tuning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.3","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:41:06.072933Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.3","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:23:44.582153Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.3","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:22:32.737574Z","created_by":"IndigoBear","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.3","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.5.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:41:10.265504Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.5.4","title":"Run original-mission operator acceptance scenarios from fresh install through long-haul recovery","description":"# Goal
Prove the original user mission in end-to-end operator terms.

This task exists because the finish line is not a set of isolated unit victories. It is a story that starts from setup, moves through normal multi-agent operation, survives errors and disconnects, and still looks trustworthy after long-haul use.","design":"# Design
1. Define acceptance scenarios that cover clean setup, environment validation, normal swarm operation, pane search and control, failure recovery, restore, incident triage, and long-haul stability checks.
2. Run those scenarios against the system with the final guardrails, setup or recovery polish, and tuning guidance in place.
3. Capture artifacts that make the result auditable by someone who did not watch the run live.
4. Record exactly what still feels rough or unsupported if any scenario falls short.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The project has end-to-end operator acceptance scenarios that directly represent the original mission.
2. Each scenario has stored artifacts and exact run procedures.
3. Success and failure criteria are explicit enough that future release candidates can repeat them.
4. Remaining gaps, if any, are documented honestly rather than hidden in a success narrative.
5. The scenario set explicitly covers clean setup, broken-environment diagnosis, standard incident triage through the new diagnostic surfaces, and recovery entry back to steady-state operation.
6.  {"id":"ft-xbnl0.5.5","title":"Assemble the final closure bundle, remaining-risk register, and release-candidate checklist","description":"# Goal
Package the finish-line work into one auditable closure bundle.

This task exists because the final state of the project should be easy to evaluate later without reconstructing the whole campaign from chat logs or scattered issue comments.","design":"# Design
1. Collect the evidence matrix, release gates, acceptance artifacts, docs updates, and major closure comments into one bead-native bundle.
2. Record any remaining risks or explicitly deferred work so the release candidate is honest.
3. Define the release-candidate checklist that future maintainers can reuse.
4. Ensure the bundle points back to the beads that generated the evidence instead of duplicating everything.","acceptance_criteria":"1. A future maintainer can evaluate the finish-line campaign from this bundle without reopening the old markdown plan.

5.  {"id":"wa-2gin","title":"Implement schema-driven docs generator (reference pages)","description":"# Task: Implement schema-driven docs generator

## Goal
Generate readable docs pages from the canonical schemas.

## Requirements
- Generate docs that include:
  - command list
  - request/response objects
  - error objects and codes
  - examples
- Output stability:
  - deterministic ordering
  - stable formatting (golden tests)

## Testing
- Golden tests for generated docs.
- CI check that generated output matches committed files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Docs pages are usable as an API reference without reading code.

-{"id":"wa-2h1ha","title":"Phase 7: Remove tokio dependency and final cleanup","description":"# Remove tokio dependency and final cleanup

## Goal

 {"id":"wa-2h5wv","title":"Refactor MuxPool (mux_pool.rs) to asupersync","description":"# Refactor MuxPool (mux_pool.rs) to asupersync

## Background
mux_pool.rs (583 LOC) wraps generic Pool for DirectMuxClient connections with WezTerm-specific health checks.

## Dependencies
- Pool refactoring (wa-36idf) must be done first
- DirectMuxClient refactoring (wa-p48pw) must be done first

## Migration
1. Thread Cx through to Pool methods
2. Update health check (list_panes()) to use Cx
3. Update connection factory (DirectMuxClient::connect) to use Cx
4. Ensure pool config works with Budget model

## Comprehensive unit tests (LabRuntime)
1. **Basic lifecycle**: create pool → acquire → use → release → shutdown
2. **Health check integration**: mock unhealthy client, verify pool replaces it
3. **Connection factory**: verify DirectMuxClient::connect called with correct Cx
4. **Idle eviction**: connections idle past timeout are evicted (virtual time)
5. **Concurrent acquire**: N tasks acquire simultaneously, max_size respected
6. **Acquire timeout**: full pool, Budget::deadline fires, clean Cancelled outcome
7. **Pool drain on shutdown**: scope exit waits for all connections returned
8. **Stats accuracy**: verify all counters under concurrent load

Each test logs: pool stats at each step, health check results, connection create/destroy events.

## Acceptance criteria
- MuxPool works with asupersync DirectMuxClient and Pool
- Health checks work through Cx
- Idle eviction works with virtual time
- 8+ LabRuntime tests
- Obligation leak oracle passes

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Pool concurrency testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test concurrent MuxPool operations:
  - Multiple tasks acquiring connections while health checks run — verify no stale connections returned
  - Interleaved acquire + release + eviction — verify pool invariants (active + idle <= max_size)
  - Concurrent shutdown while tasks hold connections — verify clean drain without orphaned connections
  - Race between health check failure and acquire — verify correct fallback to new connection creation

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for pool scaling with connection count**: Add `benches/mux_pool_scaling.rs` measuring:
  - Acquire + release cycle latency as a function of pool size (max_size=1,2,4,8,16,32)
  - Health check overhead per acquire operation
  - Pool scaling: throughput (ops/sec) with increasing concurrent task count
  - Idle eviction scan time as a function of pool size
  - Connection factory overhead (DirectMuxClient::connect latency)","notes":"CC-2 closeout 2026-04-15 (per ft-xbnl0.1.4 closure template):

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3","title":"[EPIC] Optional distributed mode: wa-agent + aggregator + wire protocol","description":"# [EPIC] Optional distributed mode

## Mission
Support multi-machine WezTerm setups by running a lightweight `wa-agent` near each remote mux server.

## Model
- `wa-agent` (remote): captures deltas/events locally and streams them to...
- `wa` aggregator (workstation): merges streams, persists to local SQLite, runs workflows.

## Guiding principles
- Keep the wire protocol small and deterministic.
- Prefer delta streaming over full scrollback dumps.
- Security by default (auth token, localhost bind unless configured).

## Definition of done
- A single remote `wa-agent` can connect to an aggregator and stream pane deltas/events.
- Aggregator persists the data and exposes it via CLI/robot/MCP.


## Success Criteria
- Distributed mode is optional, secure-by-default, and does not broaden the attack surface unintentionally.
- Aggregator/agent protocol is versioned, tested, and includes replay protection and safe bind defaults.
- Local-only mode remains the default with no required network listeners.


## Testing
- Protocol tests:
  - Encode/decode roundtrips for each message type; version negotiation behavior.

- Security tests:
  - Default bind is localhost; remote bind requires explicit dangerous flag.
  - Auth token required; missing/invalid token is rejected with stable error codes.

- E2E tests:
  - Agent→aggregator E2E validates persistence + visibility and produces artifacts (`wa-nu4.4.3.5`).

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Definition of Done.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **LabRuntime DPOR** for distributed coordination:
  - Agent-to-aggregator connection establishment: verify that concurrent connection attempts from multiple agents are handled without deadlock or dropped connections regardless of scheduling order.
  - Message interleaving from multiple agents: verify per-domain ordering is preserved even when messages from different agents arrive in arbitrary interleavings.
  - Auth token handshake races: verify that a connection attempt with an invalid token concurrent with a valid connection does not corrupt the valid session's state.
  - Aggregator shutdown with active agents: verify graceful disconnection — agents detect the shutdown and enter reconnect-with-backoff without data loss for in-flight messages.
  - Version negotiation under concurrent connects: verify that agents with different protocol versions are correctly accepted or rejected without blocking other connections.

## Cross-references
- **wa-1360** (Mux server sharding): Distributed mode's agent placement and stream routing must align with the mux server sharding topology. Each `wa-agent` maps to one or more mux server shards; the aggregator must be aware of shard boundaries to correctly attribute pane data to domains. Shard rebalancing (adding/removing mux servers) must be coordinated with agent lifecycle — the aggregator should handle agents appearing/disappearing as shards migrate. Reuse the shard identity and routing metadata from wa-1360 in the distributed wire protocol.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:39:10.220329476Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:47:47.114610Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

## Acceptance Criteria
- A future implementer can build agent + aggregator from this spec alone.
- The spec includes a concrete fixture + schema validation plan.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.2","title":"Implement wa-agent: local capture + stream to aggregator (best available capture mode)","description":"# Task: Implement `wa-agent`

## Goal
Create a lightweight daemon that runs near a mux server and streams:
- pane deltas
- detections
- gaps
- minimal metadata

## Design
- Capture engine:
  - default: polling tailers (CLI)
  - optional: streaming if vendored available
- Network:
  - connect to aggregator via configured URL
  - reconnect with backoff

## Safety
- Agent should be read-only unless explicitly given permission to perform pane-local actions.
- Network safety must follow the distributed security bead:
  - auth token handshake
  - never log tokens
  - bounded message rates / reconnect behavior

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - wire protocol encode/decode integration
  - reconnect/backoff behavior is bounded and deterministic

- Integration tests (offline):
  - stream fixture deltas into a fake aggregator endpoint
  - assert messages are well-formed and redacted where needed

- E2E coverage:
  - full agent→aggregator behavior is covered by `wa-nu4.4.3.5`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa-agent` can connect to aggregator and stream at least one pane's output deltas.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks** for capture overhead:
  - `bench_agent_capture_cpu`: Measure CPU overhead of the agent's capture loop (polling mode) at idle, moderate (1 KB/s), and heavy (1 MB/s) pane output rates. Target: <2% CPU at idle, <10% at heavy.
  - `bench_agent_capture_memory`: Measure steady-state RSS of the agent process with 1, 10, and 50 monitored panes. Ensure memory scales linearly (no per-pane unbounded buffers).
  - `bench_agent_delta_serialization`: Measure time to serialize a PaneDelta message (varying payload sizes: 256B, 4KB, 64KB). This is the hot path for streaming throughput.
  - `bench_agent_network_send`: Measure end-to-end latency from capture to aggregator receipt over localhost (baseline for distributed latency expectations).
  - All benchmarks gated behind `--features distributed`.

## macOS: Process Monitoring Considerations
- On Linux, `wa-agent` can use `procfs` (`/proc/<pid>/stat`, `/proc/<pid>/fd/`) for lightweight monitoring of mux server health and resource usage. On macOS, `/proc` does not exist.
- macOS fallbacks:
  - Use `sysctl` (`kern.proc.pid`) or the `libproc` C API (`proc_pidinfo`, `proc_pidpath`) for process introspection.
  - Use `kevent`/`kqueue` with `EVFILT_PROC` for process lifecycle events (exit, fork) instead of polling `/proc`.
  - For file descriptor counting, use `proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDLISTFDS)` instead of reading `/proc/<pid>/fd/`.
- Wrap platform-specific monitoring behind a `ProcessMonitor` trait with `LinuxProcessMonitor` and `MacosProcessMonitor` implementations. Use `#[cfg(target_os = "...")]` to select at compile time.

## Goal
Add an aggregator mode to `wa watch` that accepts remote streams and integrates them into the same pipeline as local capture.

## Responsibilities
- Accept connections from `wa-agent`.
- Merge streams (per-domain / per-pane) into the event bus.
- Persist segments/events to SQLite.
- Run workflows (and if an action must be pane-local, delegate back to the appropriate agent in future iterations).

## Security & safety
- Must enforce distributed security requirements (auth token, bind defaults, replay protection).
- Never accept unauthenticated traffic by default.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - per-pane ordering/merge behavior is deterministic
  - backpressure limits are enforced
  - malformed messages produce structured errors (no panics)

- Integration tests (offline):
  - accept a fake agent stream and ensure segments/events land in SQLite
  - ensure explicit gaps are preserved

- E2E coverage:
  - full agent→aggregator behavior is covered by `wa-nu4.4.3.5`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- With one `wa-agent` connected, `wa status` shows remote panes and `wa query` can search their output.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks** for merge throughput:
  - `bench_aggregator_merge_single_agent`: Measure events/sec the aggregator can merge from a single agent stream (varying message sizes: 256B, 4KB, 64KB payloads). Target: >10K events/sec for small messages.
  - `bench_aggregator_merge_multi_agent`: Measure aggregate throughput with 1, 5, and 20 concurrent agent streams. Identify the scaling bottleneck (CPU, SQLite write lock, event bus contention).
  - `bench_aggregator_persist_latency`: Measure p50/p95/p99 latency from message receipt to SQLite commit. Target: p95 <10ms for single-agent workload.
  - `bench_aggregator_query_under_load`: Measure `wa query` response time while the aggregator is actively ingesting from multiple agents. Ensure ingest does not starve query responsiveness.
  - All benchmarks gated behind `--features distributed`.
- **LabRuntime DPOR** for concurrent stream merging:
  - Multiple agents sending to the same domain/pane concurrently: verify per-pane sequence ordering is deterministic regardless of arrival interleaving.
  - Concurrent ingest + query: verify that a `wa query` executing concurrently with active ingest sees a consistent snapshot (no torn reads or partial segments).
  - Concurrent ingest + persist: verify SQLite write batching does not lose messages when multiple agent streams commit concurrently (test with WAL mode contention).
  - Agent disconnect during active merge: verify in-flight data is either fully committed or cleanly rolled back — no partial segments persisted.","notes":"Reopened: stalled 13-22 days with no recent activity.  {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.4","title":"Distributed security: auth token, bind defaults, replay protection (minimal)","description":"# Task: Distributed mode security

## Goal
Prevent accidental exposure when running aggregator/agent networking.

Distributed mode turns wa into a networked system; the default posture must be “safe and local”, with explicit opt-in for anything remotely reachable.

## Requirements
### Bind defaults
- Aggregator binds to localhost by default.
- Public bind requires an explicit, scary flag (e.g., `--dangerous-bind-any`) and prints a prominent warning.

### Authentication
- Simple shared auth token between agent and aggregator (v0):
  - sent in a header or the first message
  - constant-time compare
  - never logged

### Replay / injection resistance
- Basic replay protection:
  - monotonic sequence numbers per agent session
  - reject stale/duplicate sequences

- Rate limiting / connection limits:
  - cap concurrent connections
  - cap messages/sec per connection

### Redaction
- Aggregator logs and any exposed query surfaces must use wa’s redaction rules.

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests:
  - token verification rejects wrong/missing token
  - replay checks reject duplicate/stale sequence numbers

- Integration tests:
  - unauthenticated client cannot inject fake events/segments
  - authenticated client can stream
  - logs do not contain token values

## Acceptance Criteria
- An unauthenticated client cannot inject fake events/segments.
- Public bind is never the default.
- Replay/injection controls are present and test-covered.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.5","title":"Distributed E2E tests: agent→aggregator stream, persistence, CLI visibility","description":"# Task: Distributed E2E tests (feature distributed)

## Goal
Validate distributed mode end-to-end so it can be trusted when enabled:
- agent → aggregator streaming
- persistence + indexing
- CLI/robot visibility of aggregated data

Distributed mode is optional, but if we ship it, it must not be flaky.

## E2E harness requirements
- Must run **offline** with no real WezTerm required.
- Prefer a deterministic "fake agent" producer that emits:
  - segments (PaneDelta)
  - explicit GAP events
  - detections/events
  - out-of-order or duplicated messages (to test robustness)

- Spin up an aggregator instance bound to localhost with an ephemeral port.
- Capture artifacts:
  - aggregator logs
  - agent logs
  - a small exported DB snapshot

## Test runner integration
- Must run via the shared E2E runner (`./scripts/e2e_test.sh`) and follow the harness artifacts contract.
- The runner should allow skipping this case when `--features distributed` is not enabled.

## Required scenarios
1) Happy path
   - agent streams a few segments
   - aggregator persists them
   - CLI/robot query surfaces can retrieve them

2) GAP preservation
   - agent emits a gap
   - aggregator records it
   - downstream queries reflect the discontinuity

3) Robustness
   - duplicated messages do not create duplicate persisted rows (or are deduped deterministically)
   - out-of-order messages either:
     - are re-ordered safely, OR
     - are recorded as a gap + diagnostic (explicitly choose and test)

4) Security baseline
   - auth token required when configured
   - logs + responses remain redacted

## Observability expectations
- Structured logs with correlation ids:
  - stream connection/session id
  - pane id
  - message counts
- On failure, E2E script prints where artifacts were written.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`).
- Mark it as **non-default** unless `--features distributed` (or equivalent) is enabled.

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- An E2E script (or `cargo test --features distributed`) can spin up fake agent + aggregator and assert:
  - segments arrive
  - gaps are preserved
  - detections/events arrive
  - aggregated data is visible via CLI/robot query
- Failures produce actionable logs + artifacts.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative security scenario: missing/invalid auth token must be rejected and logged (redacted) with stable error codes.
  - Assert required artifacts exist (agent logs, aggregator logs, DB snapshot/export) before declaring PASS.

## Docker Compose Test Infrastructure
- The distributed E2E harness should use a `docker-compose.yml` to orchestrate the test topology:
  - **Service: fake-agent** (one or more instances): Runs the deterministic fake agent producer, configured to emit the required scenarios (happy path, GAP events, duplicates, out-of-order).
  - **Service: aggregator**: Runs `wa watch --aggregator` bound to an ephemeral port on the compose network.
  - **Service: test-runner**: Executes assertions against the aggregator's CLI/robot query interface after the agents have streamed their payloads.
- Use Docker Compose `depends_on` with health checks to sequence startup (aggregator healthy before agents connect).
- Artifact collection: mount a shared volume for logs and DB snapshots; the test-runner service copies artifacts to the host on completion (success or failure).
- Compose profiles: use `--profile distributed` so these services are not started by default (mirrors the `--features distributed` gate).

## macOS Docker Considerations
- **Docker Desktop for Mac** runs Docker inside a Linux VM, which affects this harness:
  - **Networking**: Inter-container networking works identically, but host-to-container port mapping uses the Docker Desktop VM's network stack. Use `docker compose port` to discover mapped ports rather than hardcoding.
  - **File system performance**: Shared volumes between macOS host and containers are slow. Use named volumes for the shared artifact directory and copy artifacts to the host only at the end of the test run.
  - **Apple Silicon (arm64)**: Ensure all container images in the compose file support `linux/arm64`. If any image is amd64-only, use `platform: linux/amd64` explicitly and accept the Rosetta 2 emulation overhead (document expected slowdown).
  - **Resource limits**: The default Docker Desktop resource allocation (2 CPU, 2 GB RAM) may be insufficient for running multiple agent containers + aggregator concurrently. Document minimum requirements (4 CPU, 4 GB RAM) and detect/warn at harness startup.
- CI matrix should run these E2E tests on both a native Linux runner and a macOS runner to catch platform-specific Docker behavior differences.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:41:06.121299709Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-04-06T04:07:56.916062Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T04:07:56.915851Z","close_reason":"Implemented compose-backed distributed E2E coverage and validated core/listener/bench/compose paths","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2875,"issue_id":"wa-nu4.4.3.5","author":"QuietGate","text":"QuietGate progress update (2026-04-06):

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.6","title":"Distributed mode readiness criteria + rollout checklist","description":"# Task: Distributed mode readiness criteria + rollout checklist

## Goal
Define when distributed mode is safe to enable and how to roll it out without surprises.

## Why
Distributed mode introduces network and security risks. We need explicit criteria and a checklist before enabling it by default.

## Checklist topics
- Security baseline met (auth token, bind defaults, replay protection).
- Observability present (logs, metrics, health snapshots).
- E2E coverage exists for agent→aggregator flows.
- Backward compatibility for wire protocol versions.
- Failure modes documented (agent disconnects, partial data, gaps).

## Deliverables
- A go/no-go checklist stored in this bead.
- Decision on default feature gating (off by default, explicit `--features distributed`).
- Documentation notes for users.

## Testing
- Validate that the checklist references existing tests/E2E cases by ID.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Distributed mode is blocked until all checklist items are met.
- Rollout steps are documented and self-contained.
